critic_raw:
## Critique of Benchmark Design: Streaming Platform Recommendation Stack Evaluation

This analysis focuses on structural problems with the benchmark design — issues that prevent the experiment from supporting the claim product leadership wants to make.

---

### Group 1 — Fundamental Confound: Three Variables Changed, One Number Reported

**Issue 1: Different candidate pools mean the rerankers are being evaluated on different tasks**

Claim being made: The 4.5% relative NDCG@20 gain demonstrates that the transformer reranker is a stronger ranking model than the gradient-boosted reranker.

Why this is wrong: NDCG@20 measures how well a system orders the items it was handed. The transformer receives 500 candidates from the two-tower retriever; the GBDT receives 200 from the matrix-factorization retriever. These are not the same task. The 500-item pool will contain more relevant items in expectation — not because the transformer ranked better, but because the retriever had more surface area to find relevant items. The measured gain conflates retrieval recall improvement with ranking model quality. A weaker ranker on the 500-item pool may outscore a stronger ranker on the 200-item pool if the retrieval recall differential is large enough.

The correct interpretation of the reported 4.5% is: the new end-to-end stack produces higher NDCG@20 on this evaluation protocol. That is all. The experiment provides no instrument to decompose this gain into retrieval and ranking contributions.

Evidence required: Evaluate both rerankers on both retriever candidate sets in a 2x2 design. The key cell is transformer reranker applied to the legacy MF retriever's 200-item pool — this directly tests whether the ranker contributes a gain independent of the retriever improvement.

**Issue 2: Candidate pool size increase is an independent variable that was not controlled**

Claim being made: Scaling the candidate pool from 200 to 500 is an infrastructure fact, not a variable affecting the comparison.

Why this is wrong: Giving either reranker 500 candidates rather than 200 will, for any retriever with positive precision at the margin, increase NDCG@20 ceiling. The question of whether the transformer reranker adds value beyond what the legacy GBDT would achieve with the same 500-item input is central to the leadership claim and has not been tested. The upgrade in scoring capacity is real and potentially valuable, but it is not evidence that the ranking model improved.

Evidence required: Score the legacy GBDT reranker on the two-tower's 500-item candidate pool. Compare that NDCG@20 against the reported baseline (GBDT on 200-item MF pool) and against the reported new result (transformer on 500-item two-tower pool). If most of the gap closes, the pool-size effect dominates.

**Issue 3: No factorial ablation — not a methodological justification, a gap in the evidence**

Claim being made: "Launched together in code review" explains why the factorial ablation was not run.

Why this is wrong: The ablation does not require re-deploying legacy infrastructure. Both rerankers can score a common set of candidates offline on the data the team already has. "We launched them together" explains why an online A/B test cannot isolate the components, but it does not explain why the offline evaluation was not designed with ablation cells. The offline dataset is available; the reranker scoring is independent of the retriever in inference; the ablation is feasible. Its absence is a gap in the experimental design, not an unavoidable constraint.

Evidence required: Same as Issues 1 and 2 — the 2x2 offline ablation.

---

### Group 2 — Replay Evaluation Introduces Structural Biases Against Novel Retrieval

**Issue 4: Impression log coverage gap — novel candidates are invisible to the metric**

Claim being made: NDCG@20 computed on impression logs accurately measures the quality of both systems' recommendations.

Why this is wrong: Replay evaluation scores items based on historical user interactions. Items that the two-tower retriever returns that were never shown to users under the legacy system have no click signal — they are treated as irrelevant by the metric, not because they are irrelevant, but because the historical logging policy never observed them. This is the canonical off-policy evaluation / missing-data problem in recommender evaluation.

The directional implication is important: this bias works against the new system. The 4.5% lift is likely a lower bound on true system improvement. But this also means the metric is not a calibrated estimator of real-world quality — it measures system performance on the subset of items that the legacy logging policy covered, not the full recommendation space.

Evidence required: Report what fraction of the two-tower retriever's 500-item candidate pools have any logged impressions. If >30% of candidates are outside historical impression coverage, the evaluation is primarily measuring the overlap between the two systems' retrieval sets, and the new system's broader recall is largely invisible.

**Issue 5: Position bias in impression logs implicitly rewards the legacy ranker's decisions**

Claim being made: Historical click data is a reliable relevance signal for both systems.

Why this is wrong: Items at the top of historical rankings received more clicks than items at the bottom, not purely because they were better, but because they were more exposed. The legacy system determined the display positions that generated these clicks. Items the legacy GBDT ranked first accumulated a positive click bias; items it ranked lower did not. When these biased clicks become NDCG relevance labels, the evaluation favors systems that reproduce the legacy ranker's ranking — not systems that rank by true user preference. The transformer reranker, which uses a different architecture and loss function, may consistently choose orderings that diverge from the legacy ranker's priors. This would create a systematic penalty against the new system that has nothing to do with its quality.

Evidence required: Apply position-based propensity correction (inverse propensity scoring) to the click labels before computing NDCG@20. If the corrected and uncorrected metrics diverge substantially, the raw metric is not reliable for this comparison.

---

### Group 3 — User Sample May Not Represent the Population That Motivates the Architecture Change

**Issue 6: 30-day log expiry and leave-last-week-out split combine to select high-activity users**

Claim being made: NDCG@20 on the 5% user sample generalizes to the full eligible population.

Why this is wrong: The 30-day log expiry conditions the eligible population on users with recent activity — low-frequency, lapsed, or cold-start users are filtered out. The leave-last-week-out split further requires that each user have enough history to have a "last week" hold-out period with meaningful interactions. Together, these constraints select for active, engaged users with dense interaction histories. If the motivation for switching from matrix factorization to two-tower retrieval is to improve cold-start recommendation quality (a standard motivation for this architectural choice), the evaluation excludes the user segment most relevant to testing that hypothesis.

Evidence required: Stratify NDCG@20 by user interaction frequency (e.g., quartiles of 30-day activity). If the lift is concentrated in the high-frequency quartile, the evaluation does not generalize to the cold-start users the architectural change may have been designed to serve.

---

### Synthesis: What Must Be Done Before the Leadership Claim Is Made

The 4.5% lift is real — some combination of variables in the new stack produces a higher NDCG@20 on this evaluation protocol. What the experiment cannot tell leadership is *which* variable is responsible. The architectural framing — "the new ranking model is stronger" — implies the transformer reranker deserves credit for the lift, that it should be prioritized for investment, and that the improvement would persist if the ranker were changed in isolation. None of those implications can be verified from the current experiment.

Minimum required before the leadership claim:
1. Run the 2x2 factorial ablation offline on the existing data.
2. Check whether the GBDT on the 500-item pool closes most of the gap (pool-size confound test).
3. Report impression log coverage for two-tower novel candidates.
defender_raw:
## Defense of the Benchmark Design

I will assess each critique point honestly. My goal is accurate calibration, not winning an argument.

---

### Implementation Soundness Check

Before addressing individual points: the experiment is designed to compare full stacks (legacy vs. new) on end-to-end NDCG@20. This is a valid question with a valid experimental answer. The problem is not with the experiment's design for that question — the problem is the gap between what the experiment measures and what leadership is claiming it proves. That gap is real and I cannot argue it away.

---

### Issue-by-Issue Analysis and Verdicts

**Issue 1: Different candidate pools — rerankers evaluated on different tasks**

The critic is correct. NDCG@20 is conditional on the candidate set. The two systems present different candidate sets to their rerankers. The metric cannot decompose retrieval recall improvement from ranking model improvement. The 2x2 factorial is the appropriate remedy. I have no rebuttal to the core mechanism. The experiment answers "is the full new stack better?" — not "is the transformer reranker better?"

*Verdict: Concede.*

**Issue 2: Candidate pool size as an independent confound**

The critic is correct that expanding the pool from 200 to 500 items increases the NDCG@20 ceiling for any reranker with positive retrieval precision at the margin. The test the critic proposes — GBDT on the 500-item two-tower pool — directly isolates the pool-size contribution. This experiment has not been run. The concern is mechanistically valid and not addressed by the current design.

*Verdict: Concede.*

**Issue 3: No factorial ablation**

The "launched together in code review" explanation is not a methodological justification. An offline ablation on the existing data is feasible — it requires only scoring both rerankers on a common candidate set, which is independent of the production launch. I cannot defend the absence of this analysis as a principled design decision.

*Verdict: Concede.*

**Issue 4: Impression log coverage gap and missing-data bias**

The mechanism is real. Items outside the historical impression distribution receive zero relevance credit in replay evaluation. This is a well-known limitation of offline recommender evaluation. The critic correctly notes that this bias works against the new system — the 4.5% lift is likely conservative, not inflated, because the new retriever's novel candidates are invisible to the metric.

I want to surface this implication more explicitly than the critic does: the direction of this bias makes the reported result more trustworthy in its direction (the new stack is better), even though the magnitude is uncertain. But the overall point — that the metric is not a clean quality estimator for either system — stands.

*Verdict: Concede with qualification (direction of bias favors interpreting the result as conservative).*

**Issue 5: Position bias in impression logs**

Position bias is a real and documented problem in offline recommender evaluation. The legacy system's historical ranking decisions shaped click accumulation, which becomes the relevance signal. Systems that diverge from legacy ordering are penalized by this structure independent of their quality. IPS correction is the standard remedy and has not been applied. The 4.5% lift is again observed despite this headwind — suggesting the gain is real — but the magnitude cannot be trusted without correction.

*Verdict: Concede. IPS correction should be applied.*

**Issue 6: 30-day log expiry and user selection bias**

The 30-day expiry constraint is inherent to offline replay evaluation using time-windowed impression logs — it is not specific to this experiment's design choices. Any evaluation using such logs will be conditioned on users with recent activity. The team's choice to use uniform sampling from all traffic segments is reasonable given the constraint.

However, the critic's concern about cold-start generalizability is conditionally valid: if the architectural motivation was cold-start improvement, the evaluation protocol underrepresents that user segment. The critic assumes this motivation. If the actual motivation was latency, serving cost, or recall breadth for active users, the cold-start concern is less material. The stratified analysis is good practice regardless.

*Verdict: Partial rebut — constraint is inherent to the evaluation modality, not a specific design flaw. Stratified analysis is still recommended.*

---

### Overall Assessment

The experiment is valid for comparing end-to-end stack performance. It is not valid for attributing the lift to the ranking model specifically. Issues 1, 2, and 3 are structural confounds that block the leadership claim. Issues 4 and 5 are measurement biases that work against the new system — making the directional result more robust but the magnitude less trustworthy. Issue 6 is an inherent constraint of the evaluation modality, partially rebutted.

**Verdict: critique_wins.** The 2x2 factorial ablation is the minimum required analysis before the leadership claim can be made. Its absence is the decisive gap.
adjudication_raw: Both critic (isolated, no defender context) and defender (isolated, no critic context) reach the same structural conclusion independently. The critic identifies six issues: (1) different candidate pools invalidating ranker attribution; (2) pool size increase as an untested confound; (3) absent factorial ablation not justified by code review logistics; (4) impression log coverage gap biased against novel candidates; (5) position bias in click labels; (6) active-user selection from 30-day expiry. The defender, operating independently without seeing the critique, reaches the same primary conclusions: concedes all three group-1 issues (pool confound, pool-size confound, ablation gap), concedes issues 4 and 5, partially rebuts issue 6. The independent agreement on all structural flaws is strong. Both outputs affirm that the factorial ablation is blocking. Verdict: critique_wins, with empirical test required to decompose the gain.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: Rerankers evaluated on different candidate pool distributions — different input sizes create different tasks
Issue 2: Candidate pool size increase (200→500) is an independent confound not controlled for
Issue 3: Factorial ablation absent despite feasibility on existing offline data
Issue 4: Impression log coverage gap — novel two-tower candidates receive zero relevance credit (bias against new system)
Issue 5: Position bias in historical click labels favors systems matching legacy ranker's ordering
Issue 6: 30-day log expiry and leave-last-week-out split select for active users; cold-start generalizability unverified

empirical_test.condition: 2x2 offline factorial: {legacy MF retriever 200-item pool, two-tower retriever 500-item pool} x {GBDT reranker, transformer reranker}. Additionally: GBDT reranker scored on two-tower 500-item pool as pool-size isolation test.
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer reranker applied to legacy MF 200-item pool shows NDCG@20 gain under 1.5% relative vs. GBDT on same pool; or GBDT on two-tower 500-item pool closes more than 3% of the reported 4.5% gap
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer reranker shows 2%+ relative NDCG@20 gain over GBDT when both are scored on the same frozen 200-item MF retriever pool; GBDT on 500-item pool closes less than 1% of the gap
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Both rerankers improve roughly equally when given the larger pool, suggesting pool-size explains most of the gain but the transformer shows marginal additional benefit (1-1.5%) that may or may not be worth the infrastructure cost
